Iran: Non-Conventional Threats to the U.S. Homeland

Student Intelligence Brief  |  American Interventionism
Aranda Intel
March 2, 2026
Unclassified  //  For Educational Use  //  Student Distribution
Scope Note
Iran is actively engaged in conventional military operations — ballistic missile and drone attacks against U.S. bases in Iraq and Syria, Israeli territory, and Gulf partners including Bahrain and Qatar. This briefing brackets that conventional dimension and focuses on the non-conventional threat to the American homeland: cyber operations, assassination plots, terrorist proxy networks, sleeper cells, and influence operations — tools Iran has developed precisely because it cannot match U.S. conventional military power.
Situation Overview

Iran has maintained a sustained campaign of covert, asymmetric pressure against the United States since at least the early 1980s. What has changed in the current period is scope, sophistication, and political urgency. Following the U.S. drone strike that killed Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps–Quds Force (IRGC-QF) Commander Qassem Soleimani in January 2020, Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Khamenei publicly vowed that "those who ordered the murder of General Soleimani as well as those who carried this out should be punished." That promise has driven a persistent assassination campaign targeting former U.S. officials — including plots against John Bolton and Mike Pompeo — as well as an escalating pattern of cyber intrusions, proxy terrorism, and influence operations.

Iran's non-conventional toolkit is purpose-built for the asymmetric fight. Unable to project conventional military power onto American soil, the Islamic Republic relies on four primary instruments: (1) state-directed cyber operations through IRGC and Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS) affiliated advanced persistent threat (APT) groups; (2) assassination and kidnapping plots against U.S. officials, Iranian dissidents, and perceived enemies abroad; (3) Hezbollah and proxy networks with documented surveillance and sleeper cell infrastructure inside the United States; and (4) influence operations designed to destabilize American society, interfere in elections, and amplify domestic divisions. These instruments are not mutually exclusive — they are routinely combined and calibrated to signal resolve without triggering a response that threatens regime survival.

As of March 2, 2026, the threat environment is at its most acute in decades. Operation Epic Fury — the joint U.S.-Israeli strike campaign beginning February 28, 2026 — killed Supreme Leader Khamenei and dozens of senior IRGC officials. DHS, FBI, and CISA have all elevated domestic threat postures. The Department of Homeland Security warns that while a large-scale physical attack remains unlikely in the near term, Iran and its proxies "pose a persistent threat of targeted attacks in the Homeland" and will almost certainly escalate retaliatory actions as the military situation evolves.

Key Developments
Feb. 28 – Mar. 2, 2026
Operation Epic Fury kills Khamenei and senior IRGC leadership. DHS issues immediate elevated threat advisory. FBI steps up monitoring of suspected Hezbollah and Hamas sleeper cells in major U.S. cities. No specific credible homeland plots yet publicly confirmed, but Joint Terrorism Task Forces placed on high alert nationwide.
June 22–23, 2025
DHS issues National Terrorism Advisory System (NTAS) bulletin following Operation Midnight Hammer strikes on Iranian nuclear sites. Bulletin warns of "heightened threat environment": probable low-level hacktivist cyber attacks and Iranian government-affiliated actors targeting U.S. networks; Iran also explicitly threatened to "activate sleeper-cell terror inside the United States" prior to the strikes.
Nov. 2024
DOJ charges Afghan national Farhad Shakeri with an IRGC-orchestrated plot to assassinate President Trump — the latest in a string of post-Soleimani assassination attempts. Linked to Iran's campaign to punish those deemed responsible for the 2020 killing.
Sep.–Oct. 2024
FBI indicts three IRGC cyber actors for a "hack-and-leak" operation targeting both major U.S. presidential campaigns. Separately, DOJ announces murder-for-hire charges against an IRGC Brigadier General and intelligence network members for plots against former U.S. officials. Pakistani national also charged in a foiled assassination plot against U.S. government officials.
Dec. 2023
FBI, CISA, NSA, and EPA issue joint advisory: IRGC-affiliated hackers "CyberAv3ngers" actively compromised at least 34 U.S. water and wastewater sector devices using Israeli-made Unitronics PLCs — demonstrating intent and ability to target operational technology (OT) and critical infrastructure inside the United States.
Aug. 2022
DOJ indicts IRGC member Shahram Poursafi for plots to assassinate former National Security Advisor John Bolton (2021) and Secretary of State Mike Pompeo (2022). Both operations used intermediaries and offered cash payments of hundreds of thousands of dollars — reflecting a professional, state-directed assassination infrastructure.
Operational Map: Strike Targets — Iranian Retaliatory Infrastructure

Threat Actor Landscape
Actor Affiliation Primary Threat Vector Threat Level
IRGC Cyber-Electronic Command (IRGC-CEC) IRGC / Supreme Leader Offensive cyber operations against U.S. critical infrastructure, OT systems, defense networks; coordinates state-sponsored APT groups; also directs physical assassination operations abroad Critical
APT33 (Elfin / Refined Kitten) IRGC Long-running espionage and potential sabotage of energy, aviation, and defense sectors in the U.S. and Saudi Arabia; shifting focus toward industrial control systems and OT networks; pioneered use of Shamoon destructive wiper malware High
APT34 / OilRig (MuddyWater) MOIS Espionage against government, financial services, IT, and telecom sectors; spear-phishing; supply chain intrusions; extensive Middle East and Western operations; shares infrastructure with other Iranian APTs High
APT35 / Charming Kitten IRGC-Intelligence Organization Targeting political figures, think tanks, journalists, Iranian dissidents, academics; extensive phishing; hacked both U.S. presidential campaigns (2024); known for persistent, patient social engineering operations High
CyberAv3ngers (IRGC-CEC front) IRGC-CEC Posed as hacktivists; compromised U.S. water and wastewater OT devices (2023); demonstrated intent to attack civilian infrastructure; Treasury sanctioned six IRGC-CEC officials for directing the campaign High
Hezbollah Islamic Jihad Organization (Unit 910) Hezbollah / IRGC-QF Sleeper cell infrastructure in U.S. cities (NYC, Detroit, Houston, LA, Boston); conducted surveillance of NYPD, FBI, Secret Service, military facilities, airports, and civilian landmarks; awaiting activation signal from Iran High
MOIS / IRGC External Operations Iranian state Assassination and kidnapping plots against U.S. officials, dissidents, and dual nationals; uses criminal proxies (drug cartels, biker gangs) to increase deniability; post-Soleimani campaign has driven ~30+ Western plots since 2020 High
Iran-aligned Hacktivists (e.g., Cyber Av3ngers, Homeland Justice) Loosely IRGC-directed DDoS attacks, website defacement, disinformation amplification, low-sophistication but high-volume harassment operations against U.S. targets; serve as plausibly deniable cover for state cyber operations Medium
Lone-Actor / Inspired Attackers Iran-sympathetic; may receive propaganda or minor facilitation Hardest to detect; not directed by Tehran but may be inspired by Iranian social media and regime-aligned messaging; heightened risk when Iran issues public calls for retaliation Medium

Capability Assessment: Four Threat Vectors
🔴 Cyber Operations
Iran operates one of the world's most active state cyber programs. Microsoft's 2024 Digital Defense Report documented over 2,500 major Iranian cyber operations in 2023–24, a 320% increase over previous years. The IRGC-CEC and MOIS run distinct but coordinating APT ecosystems (APT33, APT34, APT35). Historical U.S. targets have included financial sector institutions (Operation Ababil, 2012–2014), the Las Vegas Sands casino network (2014), and U.S. water and wastewater infrastructure (CyberAv3ngers, 2023). Iran has demonstrated ability to deploy destructive wiper malware (Shamoon) and ransomware. Google's John Hultquist assesses that Iran will use "plausibly deniable" ransomware and hacktivist fronts against the U.S. and Gulf partners. CISA urges immediate hardening of operational technology and industrial control systems.
🔴 Assassination & Kidnapping Plots
Iran maintains a professional, state-directed assassination infrastructure. Since Soleimani's death in 2020, Reuters documented nearly three dozen assassination or abduction plots in the West linked to Iran. Documented U.S. cases include: the 2011 IRGC-QF plot to kill the Saudi ambassador to Washington using a Mexican drug cartel; the 2021–22 Bolton and Pompeo plots; the 2024 plot to assassinate President Trump. MOIS-linked narcotrafficker Naji Ibrahim Sharifi-Zindashti ran a network that recruited Canadian Hell's Angels bikers to carry out U.S.-based killings. The increasing use of criminal proxies — drug networks, biker gangs, hired local criminals — deliberately degrades Western law enforcement's ability to attribute and prosecute operations.
🔴 Hezbollah Sleeper Cells
Hezbollah's Unit 910 — directly overseen by the IRGC-QF — maintains documented sleeper cell infrastructure in U.S. cities. Court-documented cases are deeply instructive: Ali Kourani (arrested 2017, NYC) described himself as a "sleeper cell" member and confirmed cells would be triggered if the U.S. went to war with Iran or targeted Hezbollah leadership. Alexei Saab surveilled the UN, Statue of Liberty, Times Square, Empire State Building, JFK and Newark airports, and analyzed structural weaknesses for maximum casualty attacks. A former FBI Iran-Hezbollah unit chief confirmed that as cells detected FBI surveillance in New York, Detroit, and Houston, Hezbollah deliberately relocated operatives to "blend-in" cities: Portland, Oregon and Louisville, Kentucky. All three lines — war with Iran, targeting Hezbollah leadership, and targeting Iranian interests — have now been crossed.
🟡 Influence Operations & Transnational Repression
Iran employs sophisticated information operations to interfere with U.S. democracy and repress diaspora communities. DHS's 2025 Homeland Threat Assessment found Iran attempted to "amplify divisive narratives to incite violence, influence the U.S. electorate, and degrade trust in the electoral process." IRGC-affiliated APT35 conducted a hack-and-leak operation targeting both 2024 presidential campaigns. Iran uses social media, AI-generated content, and networks of inauthentic accounts to inflame anti-Israel sentiment and deepen domestic polarization. Separately, MOIS systematically targets Iranian dissidents and dual nationals living in the U.S. — surveilling, harassing, kidnapping, and in some cases attempting to kill critics of the regime. Fourteen Western nations issued a joint condemnation of Iranian intelligence activities in July 2025.
⚠ Critical Intelligence Gap

The FBI consistently notes that known and disrupted cases represent only a fraction of actual Iranian and Hezbollah operational footprints in the United States. Iran has also begun integrating AI tools into its cyber and information operations. CISA is currently operating with reduced staffing due to ongoing governmental reorganization, creating potential gaps in critical infrastructure monitoring at precisely the highest-risk moment. Experts measure the post-strike homeland threat timeline in days to weeks, not hours.

Case Study: Austin, Texas — March 1, 2026

What happened: At approximately 1:39 a.m. on Sunday, March 1 — less than 24 hours after Operation Epic Fury began — gunman Ndiaga Diagne, 53, opened fire on patrons outside Buford's Backyard Beer Garden on Austin's West 6th Street entertainment district. Diagne first used a pistol from his SUV while circling the block, then exited on foot and continued shooting with a rifle before being killed by police within minutes of the first call. Two civilians were killed — Ryder Harrington, 19, and Savitha Shan, 21 — with 14 more hospitalized, three critically.

What investigators found: Diagne was wearing a hoodie reading "Property of Allah" over a shirt bearing an Iranian flag design. A search of his residence uncovered an Iranian flag and photographs of Iranian leaders. The FBI's San Antonio field office acknowledged "indicators on the subject and in his vehicle that indicate a potential nexus to terrorism." The investigation — led by the JTTF — is examining his digital footprint for extremist propaganda, prior social media activity, and contacts. Diagne had prior documented mental health episodes in Texas. He arrived in the U.S. in 2000 on a tourist visa, obtained a green card in 2006 after marrying a U.S. citizen, and was naturalized in 2013. Originally from Senegal, he had addresses in New York and Texas.

⚠ Investigative Caveat

As of March 2, 2026, no confirmed link to Iranian state direction has been established. The FBI has not publicly designated the attack as terrorism. Investigators are explicitly cautioning against premature conclusions.

Key open questions: Did Diagne consume Iranian or pro-regime propaganda? Was he in contact with any facilitation network? Or did the timing, dress, and symbolism reflect independent ideological radicalization — or even a mental health crisis expressed through political imagery?

The presence of Iranian symbolism is analytically significant. It is not, by itself, evidence of operational direction. Students should resist collapsing these categories.

Analytical Framework: The Lone Wolf Phenomenon

The term "lone wolf" — formally lone actor terrorism — describes an individual who plans and executes a politically motivated attack without operational direction from an organization. The National Security Critical Issues Task Force defines it as: the deliberate creation and exploitation of fear through violence or threat of violence committed by a single actor who pursues political, social, or ideological goals entirely alone. What distinguishes lone wolf terrorism from ordinary violent crime is the political dimension of the act — violence as message, not merely as harm.

The radicalization pathway typically involves four stages: (1) grievance formation — real or perceived injustice generating anger; (2) ideological framing — adopting an extremist worldview that legitimizes violence as response; (3) triggering event — a catalyst (personal crisis, major political event) that collapses the distance between belief and action; and (4) mobilization — acquiring means, selecting a target, acting. NIJ-funded research found triggering events in over 70% of lone wolf cases. A major geopolitical event — like the death of a supreme leader and the bombing of a Muslim-majority nation — can function as a sharp, immediate trigger for an already-radicalized individual.

The mental health dimension is analytically important and frequently misused. Studies consistently find lone wolf terrorists are roughly 13.5 times more likely than organized group terrorists to have prior mental health diagnoses. But mental illness is neither a sufficient explanation nor an exculpatory one. Many individuals with severe mental illness never commit violence; many lone wolf attackers without documented illness do. The coexistence of mental health history and ideological radicalization is common — and the two frequently reinforce each other. Diagne's documented mental health episodes do not resolve the question of political motivation; they complicate it.

The "stochastic terrorism" concept — developed by scholars including Mark Hamm and Ramon Spaaij — adds another layer: when political or media figures publicly amplify inflammatory rhetoric against a target group, they statistically increase the probability that a susceptible individual will act violently, even without direct incitement. Iran's own social media apparatus, regime-aligned channels, and Khamenei's public vow of revenge have all functioned as precisely this kind of broadcast environment. Tehran does not need to direct Ndiaga Diagne to benefit from the violence he may have been inspired to commit.

Iran: Non-Conventional Threats to the U.S. Homeland

Aranda Intel
March 2, 2026

Analysis & Assessment
Near-Term Assessment (Days–Weeks)

The killing of Khamenei and the degradation of IRGC command structure represent the highest-intensity trigger event in the post-Soleimani threat cycle. The central analytical question is whether surviving IRGC and Hezbollah leadership will authorize previously pre-positioned cells to act — or hold them in reserve as deterrence. A regime fighting for survival loses the "deterrent value" of holding sleeper cells back; cells become more valuable as retaliatory weapons when there is nothing left to preserve. The near-term threat is most acute in the cyber domain: Iran's APT groups can operate even in degraded command environments, and low-level cyber attacks (DDoS, defacement, infrastructure probing) require minimal central coordination. Infrastructure sectors most exposed include water treatment, financial networks, and transportation logistics. Physical threat vectors — lone-actor attacks inspired by Iranian propaganda, particularly in cities with documented protest activity — represent a secondary concern. No specific credible plots have been publicly confirmed as of March 2, 2026.

Medium-Term Assessment (Weeks–Months)

If Iran undergoes regime transition — whether toward a more moderate government, a successor hardline faction, or sustained internal instability — the threat picture becomes more unpredictable, not less. A desperate or fragmented IRGC may activate cells without centralized authorization. Hezbollah, deeply embedded in Lebanese political structures and partially degraded by the 2025 Israel-Lebanon exchange, must make independent calculations. The 14-nation Western statement condemning Iranian intelligence activities suggests improving allied coordination, but Iran's shift to criminal proxy networks (drug cartels, organized crime) significantly complicates attribution and prosecution. The medium-term cyber threat remains high: Iranian APTs operated continuously through the 2025 nuclear strikes with only temporary operational disruption.

Long-Term Assessment (Months–Years)

The structural conditions enabling Iran's asymmetric homeland campaign — diaspora networks, technical cyber talent, criminal proxy relationships, and a decades-long doctrine of "forward defense" using terrorism as foreign policy — persist regardless of who governs in Tehran. A post-Islamic Republic Iran would likely see an initial reduction in state-directed plots, but residual IRGC networks and radicalized operatives could operate independently for years. The deeper analytical problem is this: Iran's non-conventional toolkit was designed and refined precisely because the United States is conventionally dominant. Even a fundamentally weakened Iranian state retains the motive, capability, and personnel to project asymmetric violence into the American homeland. U.S. planners must account for a threat that outlasts the regime that built it.

Discussion Questions
  • Iran has repeatedly threatened to "activate sleeper cells" but has not carried out a mass-casualty attack on U.S. soil. Does this history suggest deterrence is working — or that Iran is simply waiting for the right moment? What conditions might change that calculation?
  • Iran relies heavily on criminal proxies (drug cartels, biker gangs, organized crime networks) rather than direct IRGC operatives for domestic U.S. operations. What does this operational choice reveal about Iran's strategic priorities, and what challenges does it pose for U.S. law enforcement and intelligence attribution?
  • The CyberAv3ngers operation against U.S. water infrastructure initially appeared to be independent hacktivism — but was later confirmed as an IRGC-directed state operation. What does this pattern of "hacktivist cover" tell us about how modern states conduct plausibly deniable warfare, and should it change how we define an "act of war"?
  • The U.S. government has disrupted dozens of Iranian assassination plots against former officials. Should targeted killing of foreign nationals by a state government — even adversaries like Iran — be considered a form of terrorism? How does your answer change when the target is a dissident or critic rather than a government official?
  • American interventionism scholarship often frames U.S. covert operations as distinct from terrorism. Based on what you now know about Iranian covert operations on U.S. soil, how would you evaluate that distinction? Is there a coherent line between state covert action and state-sponsored terrorism — or is the difference primarily political framing?
